w:r.w:t: MVP Master Plan · UI/UX · User Flows · AI/ML Engineering
The intelligence layer between idea and visual execution.

This document is the single source of truth for building the AnythingVisual.ai Script Intelligence Engine MVP. It covers the full product definition, all web pages and UI screens, user flows, component architecture, and the complete AI/ML engineering stack — from prompt design to fine-tuning roadmap.
AnythingVisual.ai owns the gap no platform currently fills:
Idea → Structured Narrative → Scene Intelligence → Visual Output
. We are not a script editor. We are not an image generator. We are the structured AI brain that sits between writing and video production.
We transform raw concepts into structured visual systems that power filmmaking, marketing video ads, and digital storytelling.
Three equal user segments at MVP launch:
Structure scripts, break into scenes, get shot intelligence
Manual scene breakdown is slow and inconsistent
Turn ideas into structured video outlines with visual direction
No tool bridges script writing and visual production
Transform briefs into structured ad scripts with scene logic
Agency workflows lack AI-native structured scene output
Parse raw input into clean, formatted script with act structure
Per-scene data object: shots, style, elements, objective
Card-based visual UI — the signature UX differentiator
Metadata-driven image prompt → image generation per scene
Recommendation: Next.js 14 (App Router) + TypeScript + Tailwind CSS

Next.js is the correct choice for AnythingVisual.ai for three reasons: server components allow streaming AI responses directly to the UI; the App Router enables layout-based navigation critical for the board view; and the ecosystem has the richest set of AI-native UI libraries.
Streaming, SSR, layout system, file-based routing
Type-safe scene data objects — critical for structured output
Speed + consistency; shadcn for accessible base components
Scene card reordering in board view
Native Next.js deployment, edge functions for AI streaming
Type-safe end-to-end API, co-located with frontend
Structured scene data, real-time subscriptions, pgvector for embeddings
Type-safe, pairs perfectly with TypeScript + PostgreSQL
Async AI processing pipeline, retry logic
Rate limiting, session cache, prompt cache
The MVP has six distinct screens/pages. Each serves a specific stage of the user journey.
Sign up, sign in, choose user type
Project overview, history, create new project
The core product UX — scene cards
Expanded single scene view + image gen
◈ Purpose: Convert visitors into users. Communicate the value proposition immediately.
Single-page scroll with 6 sections. Dark theme. No sidebar.
Headline: "From Raw Idea to Scene Intelligence" — large display type
Subline: "The AI brain between writing and video production"
Two CTAs: Primary = "Start Free" (→ /auth), Secondary = "Watch Demo" (opens modal)
Animated background: slow-moving grid lines with subtle green accent nodes — cinematic feel
Social proof strip below fold: "Built for filmmakers, creators & marketing teams"
Three columns, each representing a broken workflow today
Column 1 (Filmmaker): "You write a script. You manually break it into scenes. You lose hours."
Column 2 (Creator): "You have the idea. But no structure. You improvise. It shows."
Column 3 (Agency): "Client brief to concept to storyboard takes days. Should take minutes."
Below the columns: bold statement — "No platform owns: Idea → Scene Intelligence → Visual Output. Until now."
Interactive tab UI: three tabs — Filmmaker / Creator / Agency
Each tab shows animated walkthrough of that segment's specific workflow
Tabs share the same underlying engine — emphasizes the unified platform
Show the input box → processing animation → scene board in a browser mockup
Horizontal scroll or grid of five feature cards
Each card: icon, name, one-line description, visual example
Cards: Idea Structuring / Scene Segmentation / Scene Intelligence / Scene Board / Key Frame Generation
Placeholder cards at MVP: use personas or beta tester quotes
Three quote cards with avatar, name, role, quote
CTA section: "Your next project starts here" with email capture field
Footer: logo, nav links (Product, Pricing, Blog, Contact), legal links
◈ Purpose: Frictionless sign-up with user type selection that shapes their first experience.
Centered card layout. Left panel: product visual/tagline. Right panel: auth form. Two states: Sign Up and Sign In.
Step 1 — Email + password OR Google OAuth
Step 2 — "I am a..." selection: Filmmaker/Screenwriter | Content Creator | Marketing/Agency
Step 3 — "What will you create first?" quick intent: Feature Film | Short Film | YouTube Video | Brand Ad | Social Content
Step 4 — Redirect to /dashboard with onboarding modal
User type selection is stored in profile and used to: personalize dashboard copy, pre-fill example scripts, and tune default tone/style suggestions.
◈ Purpose: Project management hub. Entry point to all projects and creation.
Persistent left sidebar + main content area. Sidebar is always visible on desktop, collapsible on mobile.
Logo + nav: Dashboard, Projects, Settings, Help
User avatar + name + plan badge (Free / Pro)
"+ New Project" CTA button — always visible, high contrast
Greeting bar: "Good morning, [Name]. Ready to build something?"
Stats strip: Total Projects / Scenes Generated / Images Generated / Scripts Processed
Recent Projects grid: card per project showing title, last edited, scene count, thumbnail of first scene
Each project card has: Open / Duplicate / Delete options (3-dot menu)
Empty state (new user): Large illustrated CTA — "Create your first project" with sample project option
Top: scene thumbnail or gradient placeholder
Body: Project title (bold), "X scenes · Last edited Y ago"
Footer: User type tag (e.g. "Film") + action buttons
◈ Purpose: The entry point of the core product. Where users feed the AI their raw material.
Full-screen split layout: Left 60% = input area. Right 40% = context panel (tips, examples, recent inputs).
Large label at top: "DROP YOUR IDEA, SCRIPT, OR CONCEPT" in small caps
Textarea: full height, monospace font, dark background, subtle green caret
Placeholder text cycles through 3 examples relevant to user's chosen segment
Word/character count in bottom-right of textarea
Below textarea: Input Format selector — toggle chips: Rough Idea | Bullet Points | Partial Script | Full Script | Dialogue Fragments
Optional: Genre selector (Drama / Thriller / Comedy / Horror / Documentary / Ad / Tutorial / Vlog)
Optional: Target length (Short Form <3min / Medium 3-15min / Long Form 15min+)
Primary CTA: "Analyze & Structure →" — large, accent colored, activates on any input
Secondary: "Use Sample" — loads a pre-built example for the user's segment
Section 1: "What the engine will detect" — animated checklist: Scenes / Characters / Locations / Tone / Visual Energy / Shot Types
Section 2: "Tips for best results" — 3 collapsible tips
Section 3: Recent inputs from other projects (quick-load)
Dark overlay with centered processing card
Step-by-step pipeline: Parsing Structure → Detecting Scenes → Extracting Characters → Analyzing Tone → Generating Shot Intelligence → Building Board
Each step checks off with animation as it completes
Average processing time: 8-15 seconds for typical input
On complete: smooth transition to Scene Board
◈ Purpose: The signature UX. The structured visual brain. This is the product differentiator.
Full-width board view. Sticky header. Scrollable card grid (3 columns desktop, 2 tablet, 1 mobile). Optional full-width script strip at top.
Left: Project title (editable inline) + scene count + character count
Center: View toggle — Board View | List View | Script View
Right: Export button (dropdown: JSON / PDF / Final Draft .fdx) + Settings + Share
Collapsed by default, expandable via toggle
Shows the full cleaned script output from the AI
TipTap editor — user can edit the script inline
Changes to the script trigger a "Re-analyze" prompt: "Script updated. Re-segment scenes?"
Scene Card Design (the core UI element)
Each scene becomes a structured card. The card has two states: collapsed (default) and expanded.
Header: Scene number (SCENE 01) + Tone badge (color-coded: Tension=red, Defiance=orange, Urgency=cyan, Hope=green, Melancholy=purple)
Meta row: Location icon + location name | Clock icon + time of day
Character tags: pill badges for each character name
Scene objective: one-line summary in muted text
Footer: "Scene Intelligence ▼" expand trigger + tone color accent dot
Intelligence Grid (2x2 or 2x3 layout): Visual Style | Shot Types | Required Elements | Scene Script
Visual Style: text description of lighting, mood, camera aesthetic
Shot Types: tagged pills (Extreme Close-Up, Dutch Angle, Tracking Shot, etc.)
Required Elements: checklist of props, environment cues, special requirements
Scene Script: monospace formatted script text in dark code-style box (editable)
Key Frame Generation section at bottom (see Page 6 for detail)
Drag card → reorder scenes (updates scene numbers automatically)
"AI Refine" button per card → opens refinement sidebar for that scene
"Duplicate" button → creates copy of scene below
"Add Scene" button at end of board + between cards on hover
"Add Scene" — blank scene card with AI-assist option
"Re-analyze All" — re-runs full segmentation if script was edited
"Generate All Keyframes" — batch key frame generation (P1)
Filter bar: filter cards by character / location / tone / time-of-day
◈ Purpose: Deep scene workspace. Full intelligence view + AI refinement + key frame generation.
Two-column layout. Left 55%: scene data, script, editing. Right 45%: visual generation workspace.
Full scene metadata: all fields from the collapsed + expanded card state
Inline editable fields: click any field to edit (location, time, characters, tone, objective)
"AI Suggest" button next to each field: regenerates that specific field based on context
Scene Script editor: full TipTap editor with screenplay formatting
Scene History: version history of AI refinements (last 5 versions, restore button)
Character breakdown table: each character, lines in scene, emotional arc
Auto-generated prompt preview: "Based on scene metadata, we will generate: [prompt]"
User can edit the auto-prompt before generating
Style presets: Cinematic | Documentary | Commercial | Animated | Noir | High-Key
"Generate Key Frame" button — calls image generation API
Generated image displays with metadata overlay (scene #, title, style)
Image actions: Download | Regenerate | Use as Character Reference | Add to Storyboard
History panel: all previously generated images for this scene (thumbnail grid)
Top breadcrumb: Project Title → Scene Board → Scene [N]: [Title]
Prev / Next scene arrows — navigate between scenes without returning to board
Core Flow: Idea to Scene Board
This is the primary happy path. Every user segment follows this flow.
Loads hero section, detects segment from UTM or behavior
Shows empty state with 'Create First Project' CTA
Creates project record, routes to input studio
Smooth transition, scene board populates with cards
Card expands to show full Scene Intelligence
Calls image API, displays generated image
User is on the Scene Board after initial analysis
User opens the Script Strip by clicking the toggle
User edits the script text directly in the TipTap editor
System detects changes and shows banner: "Script updated. Re-segment scenes?"
User clicks "Re-analyze" — processing overlay runs again
New scene cards are generated; existing cards that match are preserved; new/removed scenes are highlighted
User expands a scene card on the board
User clicks "AI Refine" button on the card
Refinement sidebar slides in from the right
Sidebar shows: current scene data, refinement options (Adjust Tone / Rewrite Script / Suggest Alternative Location / Change Visual Style / Add Character)
User selects refinement type and optionally provides direction (text input)
AI generates updated scene — streams into the card fields in real time
User accepts or rejects the refinement (version history preserved)

The Script Intelligence Engine is a multi-stage AI pipeline. Each stage is a distinct processing unit with its own prompt, model call, and output schema. The pipeline is orchestrated server-side and results are streamed to the frontend as structured JSON.

The core technical insight: we do not use AI as a chatbot. We use AI as a structured data extraction engine. Every output is a typed JSON object, not free text. This is what makes scene data programmable, editable, and passable to image generation.
Accepts raw user input (idea, bullets, partial script, or full script) and normalizes it into a canonical form. Also classifies the content type, genre, and estimated length.
Claude 3 Haiku or GPT-4o-mini (fast, cheap — this is a triage stage)
System prompt instructs the model to act as a script development professional. Few-shot examples for each input type (idea vs bullets vs partial script). Output must be strict JSON.
Takes normalized input and produces a properly formatted, clean screenplay-style script. Adds act structure if appropriate. Fills narrative gaps. Ensures logical flow.
Claude 3.5 Sonnet or GPT-4o (reasoning and creativity required here)
Chain-of-thought prompting: model first outlines narrative beats, then writes the script
System prompt includes screenplay formatting rules (INT./EXT., scene headings, action lines, dialogue format)
User type context is injected: filmmaker prompts differ from ad agency prompts
Genre constraints are applied from Stage 1 output
Formatted script text (string) + detected act boundaries (array of character positions)
The most important stage. Identifies and extracts each scene as a structured data object. This is where raw text becomes an intelligence layer.
Claude 3.5 Sonnet — best performance on structured extraction tasks with complex schema
Provide the full script from Stage 2 as context
Instruct model to identify scene boundaries using screenplay conventions (INT./EXT. headers, location changes, time jumps)
Use XML output tags to demarcate each scene object — more reliable than asking for JSON directly
Then parse XML tags into JSON post-response
Temperature: 0.2 (we want deterministic extraction, not creative variance)
Takes each scene object from Stage 3 and enriches it with cinematography intelligence: shot types, visual style description, required elements, color palette suggestion.
Claude 3.5 Sonnet with a specialized system prompt trained on cinematography knowledge
Process scenes in parallel (Promise.all) — do not serialize; this is the bottleneck stage
System prompt: "You are a Director of Photography and Visual Storyteller with 20 years of experience."
Include scene object from Stage 3 as full context
Ask for specific, actionable visual intelligence — not generic descriptions
Few-shot examples calibrate specificity: bad = "moody lighting", good = "single practical source, 2700K tungsten, heavy shadow ratio 4:1, motivated by desk lamp"
Stage 5 — Key Frame Image Prompt Construction & Generation
Assembles the structured scene metadata from Stages 3 and 4 into an optimized image generation prompt. Calls the image generation API. Returns the image URL.
This is not a simple template. It is a deterministic algorithm:
Quality suffix: cinematic, film grain, anamorphic lens, 4K, professional cinematography
Negative prompt: cartoon, illustration, text, watermark, blurry, low quality
PostgreSQL via Supabase. All scene data is stored as structured JSONB for flexibility during the MVP iteration phase.
id, user_id, title, status, created_at, updated_at
id, project_id, raw_input, normalized_content, formatted_script, version
id, project_id, script_id, scene_number, scene_data (JSONB), visual_data (JSONB), created_at
id, scene_id, prompt_used, image_url, model, style_preset, created_at
id, project_id, status, stages_completed, error_log, duration_ms
Log all user edits to AI-generated scene fields (what they changed, what they kept)
Track which scene intelligence fields users engage with most
Collect user ratings on scene quality (thumbs up/down per field)
Build a labeled dataset of: raw_input → ideal_structured_output pairs
Input: script text → Output: scene JSON objects
Faster, cheaper than GPT-4o; 90%+ accuracy on segmentation
Input: scene object → Output: visual schema
Input: scene data → Output: optimized image prompt
Consistent style, better image quality, reduced prompt engineering
Embed all scene objects using text-embedding-3-large (OpenAI) or Claude embeddings
Store in pgvector (already in Supabase PostgreSQL)
Enable: "Find scenes similar to this one across all your projects"
Enable: "Suggest shot types based on similar scenes from cinematic database"
Build proprietary cinematic reference database as competitive moat
Trigger full AI pipeline on input text — streams response
Get all scenes for a project
Generate key frame image for scene
The analyze endpoint uses Server-Sent Events (SSE) to stream pipeline progress to the frontend. Each pipeline stage emits an event when it completes, enabling the animated step-by-step processing UI.
Frontend TanStack Query subscription updates the board in real time as stages complete
If a stage fails, the event includes an error and partial results are preserved
Root layout: auth state, theme, global nav
Zustand store for active project state
Full input UI, format selection, file upload
dnd-kit drag container, renders all SceneCards
Individual scene card — collapsed and expanded states
Collapsible TipTap editor for full script
projectSlice: project metadata, status, active project ID
scenesSlice: array of scene objects, ordering, selected scene
uiSlice: which cards are expanded, sidebar state, processing step, active view (board/list/script)
pipelineSlice: pipeline status per stage, error states, streaming progress
Next.js setup, auth (Clerk), Supabase schema, tRPC scaffolding, Tailwind + design system tokens
Input normalization, script structuring, scene segmentation — all returning JSON
Visual intelligence enrichment, key frame prompt assembly, image gen API integration
SceneCard component (collapsed + expanded), board layout, dnd-kit reordering
Script input UI, file upload, processing overlay animation, SSE streaming
Scene detail page, refinement sidebar, AI refine endpoint, version history
Dashboard UI, project cards, landing page, auth flow, onboarding
Export (JSON, PDF), mobile responsive, error states, performance, beta testing
User can sign up and select their user segment
User can input a rough idea, bullet points, or partial script
AI pipeline processes input and returns structured scene objects
Scene Board displays cards with full intelligence panel per scene
User can expand any scene to see visual style, shot types, elements, and script
User can generate a key frame image for any scene
User can edit any scene field and trigger re-analysis
User can export the full scene data as JSON or PDF
Users who complete processing / users who start
Users who expand at least one scene panel
Users who generate at least one image
Users who create a second project within 7 days
Pipeline runs with no error / total runs
Time from submit to board render
Primary image gen provider for MVP
Replicate (FLUX) vs fal.ai vs OpenAI DALL-E 3
fal.ai — fastest inference, best pricing, FLUX model quality
No AI limit vs 3 projects/mo vs 10 scenes/mo
10 scenes/month free — low barrier, clear upgrade prompt
Full feature parity vs read-only vs board-only
Board read-only on mobile; input + editing desktop-first
V1.1 — JSON + PDF is sufficient for MVP; .fdx is niche
Roadmap — single user for MVP, adds significant complexity
AnythingVisual.ai · Script Intelligence Engine · MVP Master Plan · Version 1.0
This document is confidential and intended for the founding team and close collaborators only. All architecture decisions are subject to revision as the product evolves.
